programs.name: Delaware Medicaid for the Elderly
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home care or daily help at home, Delaware Medicaid can cover the full cost of long-term care.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, Delaware Medicaid can cover the full cost of nursing home care or comprehensive home-based services. Because this program covers long-term care that can cost $8,000-15,000 per month privately, it provides essential financial protection for families facing care decisions.

Delaware offers three pathways: Nursing Home Medicaid for institutional care, Long-Term Care Community Services (LTCCS) for comprehensive home care, and Regular Medicaid for basic healthcare. Your parent must meet strict functional requirements — they need to qualify for nursing facility level of care, meaning they require daily assistance that can only be provided by trained professionals.

Delaware is an income cap state with a $2,417.50/month income limit for nursing home and LTCCS programs (2025). However, excess income can be placed into a Qualified Income Trust (Miller Trust) to achieve eligibility, so higher-income seniors can still qualify.

summary: Requires nursing facility level of care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must require nursing facility level of care — daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, mobility, plus cognitive or behavioral issues requiring professional supervision
otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident
Willing to accept nursing facility or LTCCS services

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Delaware ASSIST or contact your local Long Term Care unit — processing times vary but can be longer than expected.

steps.title: Gather financial documents
steps.description: Collect 3 months of bank statements, Social Security statements, pension information, and property deeds

steps.title: Get medical assessment
steps.description: Your parent will need evaluation to determine if they meet nursing facility level of care requirements

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Use Delaware ASSIST online tool or contact your local Long Term Care unit directly

steps.title: Follow up regularly
steps.description: Ask about typical approval timeframes when you apply — processing can take longer than expected

applicationGuide.processingTime: Variable — ask about typical approval timeframes when applying
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent's income exceeds $2,417.50/month, ask about setting up a Miller Trust to qualify — excess income can be placed in this trust.

tiers.name: Nursing Home Medicaid
tiers.coverage: Complete nursing facility care for as long as needed, even multiple years. Your parent keeps $50/month of income.

tiers.name: LTCCS (Home Care)
tiers.coverage: Full range of home care services for those who meet nursing home level of care but prefer to stay home. Must be 55+ and live in service area.

tiers.name: Regular ABD Medicaid
tiers.coverage: Doctor visits, prescriptions, emergency care, hospital stays. Does not cover long-term care.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Life insurance policies owned by applicant or spouse

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: U.S. citizenship or permanent residency documentation

categories.name: Medical Documentation
items: Medical records supporting need for nursing facility level care
Doctor assessments of daily living abilities

contentSections.text: Delaware is an income cap state — even $1 over the limit disqualifies your parent. However, excess income can be placed into a Miller Trust to achieve eligibility. Don't assume high income automatically disqualifies.

For married couples: if only one spouse needs care, the healthy spouse can keep up to $157,920 in assets and receive a monthly income allowance. Only the applicant spouse's income counts toward the limit.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for Delaware Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is completely exempt regardless of value. Delaware Medicaid cannot force the sale of your parent's home while they're living or if a spouse still lives there.

faqs.question: What if Dad's Social Security is $2,500/month — just over the $2,417.50 limit?
faqs.answer: He can still qualify by setting up a Miller Trust (Qualified Income Trust). The excess $82.50/month goes into the trust, bringing his countable income to exactly the limit. This is a common solution in Delaware.

faqs.question: If Mom gets approved for nursing home Medicaid, how much of her income does she keep?
faqs.answer: She keeps only $50/month for personal needs. The rest of her income goes toward the cost of care. This is significantly less than community programs in other states.

faqs.question: Can Dad get home care through this program instead of going to a nursing home?
faqs.answer: Yes, through LTCCS (Long-Term Care Community Services) if he meets nursing facility level of care, is 55+, and lives within the service area. Same income limits apply, but he can receive comprehensive care at home.

faqs.question: We're married and I'm healthy — will I lose everything if my spouse needs Medicaid?
faqs.answer: No. The healthy spouse can keep up to $157,920 in assets and may receive a monthly income allowance. Only your spouse's income counts toward the $2,417.50 limit, not yours.

faqs.question: How long does Delaware Medicaid approval take?
faqs.answer: Processing times vary and can be longer than expected. Ask about typical timeframes when you apply. Coverage can potentially be backdated up to 3 months before approval, so apply as soon as you think your parent may qualify.

programs.name: Diamond State HCBS Waiver
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication but wants to stay home instead of a nursing facility, Delaware's HCBS waiver covers personal care, respite, and assistive technology.

localEntities.name: Division of Medicaid and Medical Assistance (DMMA)

programs.intro:
If your parent needs significant daily help but wants to avoid nursing home placement, Delaware's Diamond State HCBS Waiver can cover personal care, respite services, and assistive technology at home. Your parent must qualify for Medicaid and meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment showing they need daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or managing medications).

The program is now integrated into Delaware's Long-Term Care Community Services (LTCCS) under Diamond State Health Plan Plus. Because all elderly and disabled HCBS waivers were absorbed into this system, there's no separate application — but slots may still be limited. Income must be under $2,485/month for single applicants, with assets under $2,000.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (clinical assessment showing need for daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, medication management, or significant cognitive/behavioral support)
otherRequirements: 60-month look-back period applies to asset transfers

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through Division of Medicaid and Medical Assistance after first qualifying for Medicaid — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid first
steps.description: Contact Diamond State Health Plan to apply for Medicaid eligibility — you cannot get HCBS without Medicaid qualification first.

steps.title: Request NFLOC assessment
steps.description: Ask DMMA for a Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment to determine if your parent needs nursing-level care but can be safely served at home.

steps.title: Submit financial documentation
steps.description: Provide 60 months of financial records, including bank statements, asset documentation, and proof of any asset transfers.

steps.title: Complete HCBS application
steps.description: Submit the 1915(c) HCBS Waiver application through DMMA once Medicaid eligibility and NFLOC are confirmed.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Prior HCBS waivers had limited slots and waitlists — current capacity limits under LTCCS are unclear
applicationGuide.tip: Start with Medicaid application first — you cannot qualify for HCBS without Medicaid eligibility, and the financial review takes time.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits (2026)
contentSections.footnote: Couples follow Medicaid spousal impoverishment protections with different limits

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Burial plots and prepaid funeral expenses

categories.name: Financial Records
items: Documentation of any asset transfers in past 5 years

categories.name: Medical Documentation
items: Medical records showing need for daily care
Current medication list and management needs

contentSections.text: Prior elderly HCBS waivers were absorbed into the Long-Term Care Community Services program — verify current availability as the system was recently restructured and may have waitlists.
The 60-month look-back period penalizes asset transfers — don't gift money or assets to qualify. Any transfers in the past 5 years can delay eligibility.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for this waiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from the $2,000 asset limit as long as she intends to return to it. The home won't count against her eligibility even if she's temporarily in care.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over $2,485/month?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify through other Medicaid pathways or spend-down options. Contact DMMA to review her specific situation — couples have different income rules with spousal protections.

faqs.question: Does having Alzheimer's automatically qualify her for nursing-level care?
faqs.answer: No, an Alzheimer's diagnosis alone doesn't guarantee qualification. She must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care based on her specific needs for daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, medication management, or safety supervision.

faqs.question: We gifted money to grandchildren last year — will this affect eligibility?
faqs.answer: Yes, the 60-month look-back period reviews all asset transfers in the past 5 years. Gifts can create a penalty period that delays eligibility. Report all transfers honestly during the application.

faqs.question: What services are actually covered under this waiver?
faqs.answer: Personal care assistance, respite care for family caregivers, assistive technology, and day services are covered. The specific hours and services depend on her assessed needs and the care plan developed after approval.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist for services?
faqs.answer: Delaware recently restructured its HCBS system, absorbing prior waivers into Long-Term Care Community Services. Current wait times are unclear — contact DMMA directly to check availability in your area.

programs.name: Delaware PACE
programs.tagline: All-inclusive medical and personal care for parents 55+ who need nursing home-level help but want to stay home.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs significant daily help but wants to avoid a nursing home, Delaware PACE provides all their medical and personal care under one program. Because PACE covers everything from doctor visits to prescription drugs to adult day care through one coordinated team, your parent won't need to juggle multiple providers or worry about coverage gaps.

Your parent must qualify for nursing home-level care (needing daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management) but be safe to live in the community with PACE support. If they're eligible for Medicaid, all services are free. If their income is above Medicaid limits, they can pay a flat monthly fee to access the same comprehensive care.

PACE is only available in specific ZIP codes served by two providers: Saint Francis LIFE and PACE Your LIFE. Your parent must live within their service areas and agree to receive all care exclusively through the PACE program.

summary: Not in other managed care programs
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Delaware's nursing home level of care criteria (needing daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Live in PACE provider service area
Currently living in community (not nursing home)
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicaid managed care, or hospice
Agree to receive all care exclusively through PACE

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE provider directly for intake and assessment - no standard processing timeline available.

steps.title: Contact your local PACE provider
steps.description: Call Saint Francis LIFE or PACE Your LIFE to start intake process

steps.title: Complete intake forms
steps.description: Fill out intake form and release of medical records consent

steps.title: Medical records review
steps.description: Provider reviews medical history to assess care needs

steps.title: Nursing home level of care assessment
steps.description: State conducts clinical assessment to verify eligibility

steps.title: Home visit assessment
steps.description: PACE team visits to ensure your parent can be safely maintained in community with PACE services

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified - involves multiple assessments and state approval
applicationGuide.tip: Gather all medical records before calling - the nursing home level of care assessment requires detailed documentation of your parent's daily care needs.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits for Free Services (Medicaid Eligibility)
excluded: Life insurance with face value under $1,500

offices.name: Saint Francis LIFE (Trinity Health PACE)
offices.address: Specific ZIP codes - contact provider for service area

categories.name: Identity
items: Photo ID or driver's license (to verify age 55+)
Proof of address in PACE service area

categories.name: Medical
items: Complete medical records from all doctors
Physical therapy or occupational therapy reports

categories.name: Financial
items: Income documentation if applying for Medicaid coverage

contentSections.text: Your parent cannot use any other Medicare or Medicaid providers while enrolled in PACE. All medical care must go through the PACE team exclusively.
If your parent's income is over Medicaid limits, they can still enroll in PACE by paying a flat monthly fee. Contact providers directly for private pay rates.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your mom can keep her home of any value and still qualify for free PACE services through Medicaid. The home doesn't count toward the $2,000 asset limit.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the Medicaid limit of $2,901/month?
faqs.answer: She can still enroll in PACE by paying a flat monthly fee instead of getting free services. Contact Saint Francis LIFE or PACE Your LIFE directly for private pay rates.

faqs.question: Can she keep her current doctor if she joins PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE requires using their medical team exclusively. She cannot see outside doctors or use other Medicare/Medicaid providers while enrolled in PACE.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a different part of Delaware?
faqs.answer: She'll be disenrolled from PACE if she moves outside the provider's service area for more than 30 days. PACE is only available in specific ZIP codes, not statewide.

faqs.question: How is this different from regular home health care?
faqs.answer: PACE provides all medical care, prescriptions, adult day care, and personal care through one coordinated team. Regular home health only covers specific services and requires coordinating between multiple providers.

faqs.question: What if she's already on Medicare Advantage?
faqs.answer: She must disenroll from Medicare Advantage before joining PACE. You cannot be enrolled in both programs simultaneously.
